content.problemStatement:
Chef Alice and Chef Bob are going to play their version of the social word game called Contact. First things first, let's say that the vocabulary of Alice and Bob are defined by English words A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ and B1,B2,…,BMB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_MB1​,B2​,…,BM​, respectively. Also, we define Prefix(S,i)\text{Prefix}(S, i)Prefix(S,i) as the prefix of string SSS consisting of the first iii characters. We define ∣S∣|S|∣S∣ as the length of string SSS.

Before the game starts, Alice tells Bob a word AiA_iAi​ from her vocabulary. The game proceeds as follows:


In the first move, Bob tells a word PPP from his vocabulary such that Prefix(P,1)==Prefix(Ai,1)\text{Prefix}(P, 1) == \text{Prefix}(A_i, 1)Prefix(P,1)==Prefix(Ai​,1).
In the second move, Bob tells a word PPP (which shouldn't have been used before by him) from his vocabulary such that Prefix(P,2)==Prefix(Ai,2)\text{Prefix}(P, 2) == \text{Prefix}(A_i, 2)Prefix(P,2)==Prefix(Ai​,2).
In the jthj^{th}jth move, Bob tells a word PPP (which wasn't used earlier by him) from his vocabulary such that Prefix(P,j)==Prefix(Ai,j)\text{Prefix}(P, j) == \text{Prefix}(A_i, j)Prefix(P,j)==Prefix(Ai​,j).
The game ends when Bob has made ∣Ai∣|A_i|∣Ai​∣ moves or Bob fails to make a move.
Bob is secretly aware of Alice's whole vocabulary and also, he wants to spend as much time as possible with her. Now, they'll play this game for all the NNN words in Alice's vocabulary. Note that all games are independent. You have to tell Bob what is the sum of the maximum number of moves he can make for each of the NNN games.


Input Format
The first line of the input contains integers NNN and MMM denoting the size of vocabularies of Alice and Bob, respectively.


Next N+MN + MN+M lines contain strings A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ and B1,B2,…,BMB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_MB1​,B2​,…,BM​, each in a separate line.



Output Format
Output the sum of the maximum number of moves for each of the NNN games (i.e., if Alice and Bob play the game for each word in Alice's vocabulary).
constraints: The sum of the lengths of all words is <= 10^6
All words consist of English lowercase alphabets
All words in Alice's vocabulary are distinct
All words in Bob's vocabulary are distinct
content.inputFormat: The first line contains two integers N and M, representing the number of words in Alice's and Bob's vocabulary respectively. The next N+M lines contain the words in Alice's and Bob's vocabulary.
content.outputFormat: The sum of the maximum number of moves Bob can make for each of the N games.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first word 'ac', Bob can make 1 move by telling 'ae'. For the second word 'abd', Bob can make 1 move by telling 'ae'. For the third word 'b', Bob can make 1 move by telling 'bd'.

hidden.label: general case 1
hidden.input:
3 3
abc
def
ghi
abc
def
jkl

hidden.label: general case 2

abc

ghi

abc

ghi

mno

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a Trie data structure to store the words in Bob's vocabulary. Then for each word in Alice's vocabulary, we can find the maximum number of moves Bob can make by checking the prefixes of the word in the Trie. We start by checking the first character of the word, then the first two characters, and so on. For each prefix, we check if there is a word in the Trie that starts with that prefix. If there is, we increment the count of moves. We continue this process until we have checked all prefixes of the word or we have made |Ai| moves.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N * M * max_length) where N is the number of words in Alice's vocabulary, M is the number of words in Bob's vocabulary, and max_length is the maximum length of a word.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(M * max_length) for storing the Trie
